questions.question: In a modern standalone Angular app, what is the idiomatic way to register the router at bootstrap?
options: Add `provideRouter(routes, ...features)` to the application config's `providers`
Import `RouterModule.forRoot(routes)` into the root `AppRoutingModule`
Call `RouterModule.forChild(routes)` in `main.ts`
Pass `routes` directly as the second argument to `bootstrapApplication`
questions.explanation: Standalone apps wire the router functionally with `provideRouter(routes, ...features)` in the providers array. `RouterModule.forRoot`/`forChild` are the legacy NgModule APIs, and `bootstrapApplication` takes an app config object, not a routes array.

questions.question: The Angular router matches a `Routes` array top-down, first-match-wins. What is the consequence for the wildcard `**` route?
options: It must be placed last, or it will swallow navigations meant for routes declared after it
It must be placed first so the router has a guaranteed fallback before matching
Its position is irrelevant because `**` is always evaluated after all other routes
It can only appear inside a `children` array, never at the top level
questions.explanation: Because matching is top-down and first-match-wins, `**` matches any URL, so any route declared after it is unreachable. It must go last. Position is not automatically reordered, and `**` is valid at the top level.

questions.question: A `User` component is reused in place (not recreated) when the URL changes from `/users/1` to `/users/2`. The component reads the id once via `route.snapshot.paramMap.get('id')` in its constructor. What happens?
options: The displayed id stays `1` after navigating to `/users/2`, because `snapshot` is a one-shot read taken at construction
The displayed id updates to `2`, because `snapshot` is a live proxy that re-emits on every navigation
Angular throws a runtime error because `snapshot` cannot be read in a constructor
The component is forcibly recreated, so the constructor runs again and shows `2`
questions.explanation: `snapshot.*` is a one-shot read, fine only when the component is recreated per navigation. With in-place reuse, the constructor doesn't run again, so the stale `1` remains. The observable `paramMap` (or `params`) is the safe default since it emits on change.

questions.question: With `withComponentInputBinding()` enabled, a route has a path param `id`, a query param `id`, static `data: { id: ... }`, and a resolver producing `id`. Which source wins when binding to the component's `id` input?
options: The resolved data, because priority is query params < path/matrix params < static data < resolved data
The query param, because query params are applied last and override everything
The path param, because URL segment params always take precedence over data
It is non-deterministic; Angular logs a warning and binds `undefined`
questions.explanation: On a key collision the priority (lowest to highest) is query params < path/matrix params < static `data` < resolved data, so resolved data wins. Query params have the lowest priority, not the highest.

questions.question: Why does an empty-path default redirect `{ path: '', redirectTo: 'home', pathMatch: 'full' }` require `pathMatch: 'full'` rather than the default `'prefix'`?
options: With `'full'` the empty path matches only when the entire URL is empty; `'prefix'` would match the leading segment of every URL and redirect everything
`'full'` is required for all redirects regardless of path; `'prefix'` is never valid with `redirectTo`
`'full'` makes the redirect run after guards, while `'prefix'` runs it before, which is unsafe for `''`
`'prefix'` only works with the wildcard `**`, so an empty-path route must use `'full'`
questions.explanation: An empty path with the default `'prefix'` matches the leading segment of any URL, so it would redirect every navigation. `'full'` requires the whole URL to be empty, so `''` redirects only the root. `'prefix'` is perfectly valid for non-empty redirect paths.

questions.question: Which statements about the modern functional guard API are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A functional guard can call `inject()` directly because guards run inside an injection context
The class-based guard interfaces such as `CanActivate` were deprecated in v15.2
Functional guards are tree-shakable and trivially composable
Functional guards cannot reuse logic that lives in a service class
A guard must return only a synchronous `boolean`; observables and promises are not allowed
questions.explanation: Guards run inside an injection context (so `inject()` works), the class interfaces were deprecated in v15.2, and functional guards are tree-shakable and composable. They CAN reuse service-class logic via `inject()`, and they may return `boolean | UrlTree | RedirectCommand` or an `Observable`/`Promise` of those.

questions.question: A `CanMatchFn` returns `false` for a lazy route. How does this differ from a `CanActivateFn` returning `false` for the same route?
options: `CanMatch` false makes the router skip this route and try the next match, and the lazy chunk is never fetched; `CanActivate` false cancels the navigation after matching
Both cancel the whole navigation identically; the only difference is when they run
`CanMatch` false fetches the chunk but blocks activation; `CanActivate` false prevents the fetch
`CanMatch` false redirects to the wildcard route, while `CanActivate` false redirects to `/`
questions.explanation: `CanMatch` runs during matching: returning `false` skips the route and lets the router try the next match, and crucially the lazy bundle is never fetched. `CanActivate` false cancels the in-flight navigation after the route has already matched.

questions.question: For a single in-flight navigation, in what order does the router run these phases?
options: `CanMatch` (match time) â†’ `CanDeactivate` (outgoing) â†’ `CanActivateChild` â†’ `CanActivate` â†’ resolvers
Resolvers â†’ `CanActivate` â†’ `CanActivateChild` â†’ `CanDeactivate` â†’ `CanMatch`
`CanActivate` â†’ `CanActivateChild` â†’ `CanMatch` â†’ resolvers â†’ `CanDeactivate`
`CanDeactivate` â†’ `CanMatch` â†’ resolvers â†’ `CanActivate` â†’ `CanActivateChild`
questions.explanation: `CanMatch` runs first at match time; then for the in-flight navigation `CanDeactivate` on the outgoing route, `CanActivateChild`, then `CanActivate`; resolvers run only after all guards pass.

questions.question: A `CanActivateFn` wants to cancel the current navigation AND redirect the user to `/login`, preserving the intended URL. What should it return?
options: A `UrlTree` built with `router.createUrlTree(['/login'], { queryParams: { returnUrl: state.url } })`
`false`, then call `router.navigate(['/login'])` on the next line
The string `'/login'`, which the router interprets as a redirect target
`new RedirectCommand()` with no arguments, which defaults to `/login`
questions.explanation: Returning a `UrlTree` both cancels the navigation and redirects in one step. Returning `false` only cancels (the line after a `return` never runs anyway), a bare string is not a valid `GuardResult`, and `RedirectCommand` requires an explicit target/options.

questions.question: A `ResolveFn` runs before activation and the router blocks the navigation until it completes, so the component renders with its resolved data already available (no flash of empty state).
questions.explanation: Resolvers run after guards pass and before activation; the router waits for them to complete, so the component renders with data ready. The result lands in `ActivatedRoute.data` under its key (or binds to an input with `withComponentInputBinding()`).

questions.question: What is the difference between `loadComponent` and `loadChildren` on a route?
options: `loadComponent` lazily loads a single standalone component for one route; `loadChildren` lazily loads a child `Routes` array for a whole feature area
`loadComponent` loads eagerly at boot; `loadChildren` loads lazily on navigation
`loadComponent` works only with NgModules; `loadChildren` works only with standalone components
They are interchangeable aliases that both load a single component
questions.explanation: `loadComponent` is the cleanest lazy unit for a single standalone component; `loadChildren` loads an entire child `Routes` array (a feature with nested pages). Both load lazily on navigation, and `loadComponent` is for standalone components specifically.

questions.question: In a custom `PreloadingStrategy`, what is the role of the `load` callback passed to `preload(route, load)`?
options: Calling `load()` triggers the actual fetch of the route's lazy chunk; returning `of(null)` skips preloading it
`load()` returns the already-downloaded chunk synchronously; the return value only logs analytics
`load()` registers the route for eager loading at the next app bootstrap, not now
`load()` must always be called or the route becomes permanently unreachable
questions.explanation: `load()` triggers the background fetch of the lazy chunk; returning `of(null)` (without calling it) skips preloading that route. The route is still loaded normally on navigation if never preloaded, so it never becomes unreachable.

questions.question: When should you reach for `@defer` instead of route-level lazy loading (`loadComponent`/`loadChildren`)?
options: When you want to lazily load a part of one page (e.g. a heavy widget on viewport/interaction/idle), since `@defer` splits within a template
When you want to split an entire page by URL, since `@defer` is the only URL-based splitter
When you need server-side rendering, which route lazy loading cannot support
Always â€” `@defer` fully replaces route lazy loading in modern Angular
questions.explanation: Route lazy loading splits by URL (whole pages); `@defer` splits within a template, ideal for a heavy widget loaded on viewport/interaction/idle. They are complementary, not replacements, and `@defer` is not URL-based.

questions.question: Which characteristics correctly describe reactive forms (as opposed to template-driven)? (Select all that apply.)
options: The control tree is constructed explicitly in the component class and read synchronously
Validators are declared in the class via `ValidatorFn`/`Validators`
Form logic is plain TypeScript that can be unit-tested with no rendered DOM
The source of truth lives in the template via `[(ngModel)]`
Custom validators are added as directives in the template
questions.explanation: Reactive forms build the control tree in the class (synchronous), declare validators in the class, and are testable as plain TS. Putting the source of truth in the template via `ngModel` and using validator directives are template-driven traits.

questions.question: In a template-driven form, the full control tree may not be available synchronously immediately after the view initializes, because the `ngModel`/`NgForm` directives wire up the controls over one or more change-detection cycles.
questions.explanation: Template-driven controls are created asynchronously by directives across change-detection cycles, so the model isn't fully available immediately. Reactive controls, by contrast, are constructed up front and readable synchronously in the same tick.

questions.question: Regarding strictly-typed reactive forms (v14+), which statements are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: By default a control's value type includes `null` because `reset()` sets the value to `null`
`group.value` is a `Partial` because disabled controls are omitted
`getRawValue()` returns every control's value with the full, non-partial type
`nonNullable: true` makes the type include `null` and causes `reset()` to set `undefined`
Disabled controls are always included in `group.value`
questions.explanation: By default the value type is `T | null` (because `reset()` nulls it), `value` is a `Partial` that omits disabled controls, and `getRawValue()` returns the full non-partial shape. `nonNullable: true` removes `null` and makes `reset()` restore the initial value; disabled controls are dropped from `value`.

questions.question: A control is configured with `{ validators: [Validators.required, Validators.minLength(8)], updateOn: 'blur' }`. When do its validators run and its value emit?
options: Only when the field loses focus (on blur), not on every keystroke
On every keystroke, because validators always run on change regardless of `updateOn`
Only when the surrounding form is submitted via `(ngSubmit)`
Never automatically; you must call `updateValueAndValidity()` manually each time
questions.explanation: `updateOn: 'blur'` makes the control commit its value and run validation only when the field loses focus. The default `'change'` runs them on every value change; `'submit'` defers until form submit.

questions.question: A `passwordsMatch` validator compares two sibling controls (`password` and `confirm`). Where must it be attached, and what is the control's status while an `AsyncValidatorFn` is running?
options: On the parent `FormGroup`; the control's status is `PENDING` while an async validator runs
On the `password` control directly; the status is `INVALID` until the async validator resolves
On each child control separately; the status is `DISABLED` during async validation
On the parent `FormGroup`; the status is `VALID` until the async validator explicitly fails
questions.explanation: Cross-field validators must go on the parent `FormGroup` so they can read both siblings via `group.get(...)`. While an `AsyncValidatorFn` is in flight, the control's status is `PENDING`, resolving to `VALID`/`INVALID` afterward.
